$comment: Test vectors for Unseen v1 cryptographic protocol. Frozen protocol artifact:
SHA-256(JSON.stringify(vectors)) is recorded as FROZEN_SHA256_OF_VECTORS in
shared/src/protocol/freeze.ts and MUST NOT change. Any change in protocol behavior
(algorithms, inputs, derivations, wire layout) requires a protocol version bump and
deliberate update of the pinned hash.
All hex values are lowercase. All HKDF derivations use SHA-256.
Validation and rehash: run bun:test for shared (the protocol-freeze suite recomputes
the hashes and asserts byte-equality against freeze.ts).

v1_hkdf_derivation.$comment: HKDF-SHA256 derivation from URL fragment secret. salt=empty (0 bytes); RFC 5869 then substitutes a 32-byte zero salt internally.

v2_x25519.$comment: RFC 7748 §6.1 reference vectors. Public, fixed. Anchored to the RFC 7748 §6.1 constants AND re-derived from private scalars (pub = X25519(priv, base), ECDH both directions) in validate-test-vectors.test.ts.

$comment: session_key derived from shared + sas_anchor with canonical transcript.
Canonical transcript ordering: bytes-lexicographic sort of [alice_pub, bob_pub].
alice_pub starts with 0x85, bob_pub starts with 0xde → alice_pub < bob_pub.
Therefore transcript = alice_pub || bob_pub (lower byte sequence first).
HKDF info = utf8(info_prefix) || transcript (21 + 64 = 85 bytes).

v4_sas_bytes.$comment: SAS bytes derived from session_key + sas_anchor. 5 bytes interpreted as uint8 each, indexing sas-emoji-v1.json pool.

v5_aes_gcm_message.$comment: AEAD encrypt of a sample v2 envelope with counter=1, direction=0x01. v2 envelope drops the `v` field and orders properties as `kind,body,t`. AAD = utf8("unseen:v1:") || 0x00 (RELAY_KIND_MSG) — 11 bytes — binds the frame kind into the AEAD so a flipped kind byte fails authentication.

v6_wire_format.$comment: Full binary RELAY frame for the v2 message from v5. v2 layout: [0x05 MSG_RELAY][kind:1][nonce:12][ct_len:u16 LE][ciphertext]. The kind byte at offset 1 distinguishes plaintext envelopes (0x00) from binary file chunks (0x01) and is bound into the AEAD AAD.

$comment: AES-256-GCM encrypt of an ephemeral X25519 public key with handshake_key (V1) and
aad = utf8("unseen:v1:handshake"). Plaintext = alice_pub from V2 (RFC 7748 §6.1).
Nonce is a fixed test value, not the runtime randomly-generated one — this is purely
for cross-implementation byte-equality verification of the handshake encrypt path.
PRF-mode wrap_key derivation. HKDF-SHA256 with prf_output as IKM, empty salt,
info = utf8("unseen:v1:wrap") || roomId_bytes (16 raw bytes from V1, NOT hex).
prf_output here is a fixed test fixture (0xCC × 32) standing in for the value
returned by an authenticator's PRF extension at runtime. This vector locks in the
exact concatenation format (raw bytes, not hex) and salt semantics (empty buffer).
PRF+PRF hardened rekey. Both peers generate fresh X25519 pairs, exchange pubs over
the encrypted RELAY channel, and derive a new session_key. Unlike V3 the HKDF info is
the bare constant utf8("unseen:v1:rekeyed-session-key") with NO transcript; salt is the
same sas_anchor from V1. Keys here are fixed test fixtures (initiator_priv=0x11*32,
joiner_priv=0x22*32) standing in for runtime-random ephemerals, locking in the byte
output of the rekey derivation for cross-implementation verification.

$how_to_verify: Run the shared workspace tests: `bun test shared`. The protocol-freeze suite re-hashes
JSON.stringify(vectors) and asserts it equals FROZEN_SHA256_OF_VECTORS in
shared/src/protocol/freeze.ts. The crypto-vectors suite re-derives every output via Bun
Web Crypto and asserts byte-equality with the values stored above.
HKDF-SHA256 — crypto.subtle.deriveBits with { name: 'HKDF', hash: 'SHA-256', salt, info }
X25519      — V2 is RFC 7748 §6.1 reference, anchored to the RFC constants and re-derived
from private scalars (pub from priv, ECDH from scalars) in validate-test-vectors.test.ts
AES-256-GCM — crypto.subtle.encrypt with { name: 'AES-GCM', iv, additionalData }, 16-byte tag
Independent verification (optional): re-compute any output with an unrelated tool
(OpenSSL CLI, Python cryptography, libsodium, etc.) using the same inputs. Bytes
must match — these are protocol invariants.
